title: AI Dev 26 x SF | Luke Kim: The Agent Data Stack—Why Every AI Agent Needs Its Own Data Stack
transcript: Hi everybody. Uh my name is Luke Kim. I'm the founder and CEO of Spice AI. Uh we are a data and AI infrastructure startup out of Belleview. Uh we build an open source project uh which is a data platform for AI context. Um which I will show you soon. So if you think about the SAS era uh of the last 10-15 years uh we had this modern data stack and you would have these centralized data systems where your apps and your dashboards, notebooks, services would all query data out of uh and you would do a whole bunch of ETL into these data sources and that worked really great. um we have a whole bunch of really great data systems built on that and especially works well for analytical data but now we're moving from the SAS era into the AI agent era and in the AI agent era we have agents and data is everything you heard from the last talk context is everything and for these agents to be useful and accurate they need data wherever it lives across the enterprise across the organization And that means not just analytical data. It means it needs access to real-time data in OLTP databases, document DBs, message buses alongside their uh your uh analytical data and the model of ETL uh doesn't work as well because now every team across every organization is being asked to build uh agentic use cases and previously you would go to your data team and say hey we need some data uh and they might build you some ETL pipelines uh and that could take weeks or months but now we're at the speed where we have to deliver these AI use cases uh as fast as possible and for these to be accurate we have to access that data wherever it lives and so we need a better data stack for agents. Furthermore uh we have this other problem which is we don't just have a couple of humans uh or a couple of dashboards or a couple of apps anymore. we have uh a whole bunch of agents uh and then we have more agents and these don't sleep. They work 247. They're often on loops and they are quering all of your data sources uh which can really become overwhelmed. And so you have more and more and more and more agents that need all the data across your organization to be useful and accurate. Uh and so the current way the current modern data stack uh simply just can't keep up. So, GitHub has been in the news a lot or on Twitter a lot this weekend in the last couple of months. Uh, they've had a bunch of outages. Um, and part of the reason is uh they just did a postmortem of it is that they've had this massive growth uh especially in the last quarter. And this growth is really been driven from agentic use cases. It's just um orders of magnitude more load on their system. And so this comes from agents and agents and agents. And so what we're finding is across the organizations your infrastructure layer that serves data to the agents which is critical uh really it needs a new way to work to actually serve all of these uh new workloads and these new query patterns. In addition other people have been in the news. Uh so uh this uh tweet had gone kind of viral where uh someone had pointed out that an AI agent destroyed their production data um so was able to get access to their database and um destroy their data. Uh and then lovable was in the news recently as well um with a security incident because they didn't have all the right controls on their backend databases. And so not only do we have to worry about uh getting data fast to agents and making sure that it can keep up with the load, we have to really make sure it's secure uh so that um it you know these agents don't do bad things and then don't have more access to data than they actually need. Uh and so it's really hard to set up all these systems and make sure did did you really get that one policy right so your agent didn't get access to everything it it needs. Uh and so really, you know, what we want to do is humans don't let humans give agents uh direct access to databases and data systems. Um we don't want to we want to give it direct access. Um we want to have a better way to do this. And so we propose that every agent gets its own data stack. And so um these this data stack uh can have secured firewall access to all of the different data systems across the organization. uh and instead of a bunch of uh ETL pipelines over into a centralized system uh you have a more distributed nature where each one of these agents can access data in a more federated way. Now that's not to say that the ETL and the pipelines and the analytical systems are not useful. They're super useful still have their place. But what we find is that this demand for agent data essentially new has new needs new ways of working. And so can we essentially give each one of these agents their own verticalized data stack where they can access their own data but it's also secured and isolated and firewalled from uh uh direct access to the you know backend data systems. So maybe we can go from this where we have agents going across and accessing all these data systems to uh giving them each uh their own data stack. Um, and in our world it can be a sidec car or something that rides alongside the agent which provides it a secure local set of data that is intentionally provisioned just for that agent and you never give it access even to the network or essentially to to those backend data systems. And so that's essentially what we've built at SPICE. It's a open-source AI native agent data stack. It provides uh full federated SQL query across all of your backend data stores. Everything from uh Parket to Iceberg to Snowflake to MySQL to MongoDB to elastic search.
